name: Equirectangular
summary: One of the oldest and simplest map projections, mapping meridians to evenly spaced vertical lines and parallels to evenly spaced horizontal lines. The whole family is defined by a single parameter: the standard parallel, which is the latitude at which the map is drawn at true scale. Named members include the Plate Carrée (standard parallel 0°, the most common format for storing raster geographic data), Marinus of Tyre (36°), the Gall isographic (45°), and Ronald Miller's minimum-distortion variants (37.5°, 43.5°, and 50.5°).

name: Mercator
summary: A cylindrical projection designed for nautical navigation that preserves local angles and shapes (conformality). Any straight line on the map is a line of constant bearing (rhumb line), making it invaluable for marine charts. Its extreme area distortion near the poles has made it controversial for world maps, but it remains the basis for most web mapping tiles.

resources.title: Why Are We Still Using Mercator?

name: Orthographic
summary: Depicts a hemisphere of the globe as it would appear from infinite distance, resembling a photograph of the Earth from space. It provides a natural and intuitive perspective view, but only one hemisphere is visible at a time and distortion increases rapidly toward the edges.

name: Vertical Perspective
summary: A generalization of the orthographic projection that simulates the view from a finite distance above the Earth. Often called the "near-side perspective" projection, it produces views similar to satellite or aerial photography. Used extensively by NASA and in television graphics to show realistic views of Earth. The Viewpoint Distance control moves the camera from close above the surface out to a near-orthographic distance.

name: Azimuthal Equidistant
summary: Preserves both distance and direction from the center point to every other point on the map. This makes it ideal for showing airline routes, radio transmission ranges, and seismic distances. The emblem of the United Nations features an azimuthal equidistant projection centered on the North Pole.

name: Stereographic
summary: Projects the sphere from a point on the surface to a tangent plane at the antipodal point. It is the only map projection that is both conformal and azimuthal, making it essential for polar maps, crystallography, and complex analysis. The Universal Polar Stereographic (UPS) system uses it for mapping high-latitude regions.

name: Sinusoidal
summary: A pseudocylindrical equal-area projection where the parallels are equally spaced horizontal lines and the meridians are sinusoidal curves. It accurately represents area at the cost of shape distortion, especially near the map edges, and is widely used in global climate and vegetation datasets where preserving area is essential.

name: Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area
summary: Preserves area while also being azimuthal, meaning directions from the center are correct. It is widely used for continental and hemispheric maps where accurate area representation is important. The European Environment Agency and many national atlases use it as their standard projection.

name: Gnomonic
summary: The oldest known map projection. It projects from the center of the Earth, causing all great circles to appear as straight lines. This unique property makes it indispensable for planning the shortest routes in aviation and maritime navigation, though it can only show less than a hemisphere and distortion grows extreme near the edges.

name: Mollweide
summary: A pseudocylindrical equal-area projection that maps the entire globe into an ellipse. It balances shape and area distortion more evenly than the sinusoidal projection, making it a popular choice for world maps showing distributions of phenomena like population, climate, or ocean currents.

name: Central Cylindrical
summary: A simple compromise projection that maps meridians to vertical lines and parallels to curves. While rarely used for cartographic purposes due to having worse distortion and less ideal properties than Mercator, it has found applications in the display and generation of panoramic images and visualization of images on very large screens.

name: Lagrange
summary: A rarely used projection that maps the sphere to a square. While it is conformal, it has significant distortion at the edges. It has been used in some artistic and thematic maps where the square format is desired.

name: Aitoff
summary: A compromise projection that maps the sphere to an ellipse. While it is neither conformal nor equal-area, the scale is correct along the equator and at the central meridian. It is often used for world maps where a balance between shape and area distortion is desired.

name: Hammer (Hammer-Aitoff)
summary: An equal-area version of the Aitoff projection that maps the sphere to an ellipse. It preserves area while still providing a visually appealing balance of shape distortion, making it a popular choice for thematic world maps.

name: Trapezoidal
summary: One of the earliest Renaissance map projections, where parallels are equally spaced horizontal lines and meridians are straight lines that bend at the equator. Historically used for regional maps where the trapezoidal shape arises naturally between two standard parallels. Extended here to a global form with flat polar edges and triangular tips on either side of the equator, which exaggerates polar distortion in exchange for a more recognizably trapezoidal silhouette.

resources.title: Renaissance Cartography and the Donis Projection

name: Goode Homolosine
summary: A composite equal-area projection that uses the sinusoidal projection at low latitudes (within roughly ±40°44') and the Mollweide projection at higher latitudes, joining the two halves where their y-coordinates match. Most frequently presented in an interrupted form that splits the oceans to reduce distortion of landmasses.

name: HEALPix
summary: An equal-area projection devised for pixelizing the celestial sphere. A Lambert cylindrical equal-area band covers the equatorial zone (where the sine of the latitude lies within ±2/3), while each polar cap is split into four interrupted Collignon facets that taper to the poles, giving HEALPix its distinctive stepped outline. Because every facet preserves area, the whole map is equal-area and subdivides into equal-area, isolatitude pixels — the property that made it the standard grid for all-sky astrophysical data such as the cosmic microwave background. This projection realizes the HEALPix grid introduced by Górski et al.

resources.title: Mapping on the HEALPix Grid (Calabretta & Roukema, 2007)

name: Cylindrical Equal Area
summary: A cylindrical projection that maps meridians to equally spaced vertical lines and parallels to horizontal lines spaced by the sine of the latitude — making it equal-area, so every region takes up map space proportional to its true area. The whole family is defined by a single parameter, the standard parallel (the latitude of true scale), which scales the map horizontally by the cosine of that parallel and vertically by its reciprocal. Because that trade-off is purely a change of aspect ratio (width:height = π·cos² of the standard parallel), the named members differ only in proportion: the Lambert cylindrical equal-area (standard parallel 0°, the base form shown here), Behrmann (30°), Hobo–Dyer (37.5°), the Gall–Peters or Gall orthographic (45°), and Balthasart (50°). Rather than a dedicated parameter, Flatsphere reaches these with the display aspect-ratio control — narrowing the default Lambert view to half its width, for example, gives the familiar Gall–Peters proportions.

name: Winkel-Tripel
summary: A compromise projection introduced by Oswald Winkel in 1921, formed as the arithmetic mean of the equirectangular and Aitoff projections. Its name — 'Tripel' is German for 'triple' — reflects Winkel's aim of simultaneously minimizing distortion in area, direction, and distance rather than eliminating any single one. The equirectangular half uses a standard parallel of arccos(2/π) ≈ 50.46°. The poles map to lines rather than points, and the gently curved meridians give a balanced, familiar appearance that led the National Geographic Society to adopt it for its reference world maps in 1998. The forward projection is closed-form, but the inverse has none; Flatsphere recovers it with Newton's method seeded by the closed-form inverse of its Aitoff half.
